data.title: Baby boom or bust: Trip.com co-founder launches $HK500 million fund to boost birth rates
data.description:
James Liang, co-founder of Chinese online travel giant Trip.com Group, has launched a HK$500 million (US$64.23 million) foundation in Hong Kong to tackle China’s deepening fertility crisis.
Citing Hong Kong’s unique advantages as a global hub that facilitates the exchange of international expertise, he said the city was the ideal springboard for tackling China’s mounting demographic pressures.
“The challenge of low fertility rates faced by Hong Kong society is highly representative, and the foundation’s pilots provide valuable reference for mainland first-tier cities and other similar Asian metropolises,” he told the Post in a written reply.
Established in Liang’s personal capacity and unveiled on World Children’s Day, the Genovation Foundation plans to disburse the funds over five years through three main channels: direct financial support for doctoral students who have children; grants of up to HK$1 million for fertility-friendly cultural and artistic projects; and a global forum that will bring together leading universities to address declining birth rates.
In its pilot phase, the foundation will offer a HK$50,000 childcare and education subsidy to full-time PhD students at Hong Kong universities whose children are born on or after January 1, 2026.
The scheme is intended to plug a gap for young academics who face the competing pressures of career advancement, limited income and high childcare costs at the start of their professional lives.
The foundation said it aims to complete applicant reviews within 14 working days and disburse funds within five working days after approval.
Liang, a scholar-entrepreneur who holds a PhD in economics from Stanford University, has for years been sounding the alarm over China’s demographic challenges.
In Wednesday’s announcement, he warned that a shrinking younger population would undermine the country’s creativity and long-term economic vitality and said that mitigating the decline in birth rates required joint action by the public and private sectors.
China’s population is projected to fall sharply over the rest of the century. Under the United Nations’ baseline scenario, it was expected to decline by roughly a factor of 2.2 to about 633 million by 2100, from around 1.4 billion today.
Hong Kong faces its own acute challenges, with high living and education costs discouraging couples from having children. The city’s total fertility rate stood at 0.772 births per woman in 2021, among the lowest in the world.

Alongside the PhD subsidies, the foundation has launched a Fertility-Friendly Cultural Innovation Project, inviting individuals and organisations worldwide to submit creative works that encourage childbearing, convey family warmth and reduce anxiety around parenthood. Annual winners can receive up to HK$1 million in prize money.
Genovation’s flagship initiative will be the Global Fertility Crisis Forum in Hong Kong in January 2026, co-hosted by Peking University, the University of Hong Kong and Princeton University.
The forum will examine demographic trends, the economic impact of low fertility, policy responses and how to balance women’s reproductive choices with their career development.
According to the foundation, its name combines “Gene” and “Innovation” to symbolise both the continuity of life and the search for creative solutions to urgent social problems.
Shanghai-based Trip.com was first listed on the Nasdaq in 2003, followed by a secondary listing on the Hong Kong stock exchange in 2021. In Hong Kong, its shares traded at HK$554 at Thursday’s close, giving it a market capitalisation of about HK$369 billion.
Founded in 1999, the online travel platform has since grown into one of the world’s largest.
In Hong Kong, Trip.com shares were trading at HK$554 on Thursday afternoon, valuing the company at about HK$369 billion.
Liang’s holdings in Trip.com, Tongcheng Travel, and Homeinns Hotel have a combined market value of US$1.2 billion, according to Bloomberg data.
Trip.com, which employs more than 30,000 people worldwide, has itself earmarked 1 billion yuan (US$140.5 million) to subsidise employee births and since 2023 has offered 50,000 yuan to each staff member who has a child.

data.title: Child kissing scandal in Indonesia reignites debate on preacher regulation
data.description:
Calls to certify Islamic preachers in Indonesia are growing after a cleric was filmed kissing young girls during religious gatherings in East Java, reigniting debate over how the state should regulate religion in the world’s largest Muslim-majority nation.
The uproar has sharpened a long-standing divide between those who believe certification is needed to protect congregants and uphold ethical standards, and critics who fear it could curb religious freedom and turn influential preachers into extensions of political power.
The preacher at the centre of the controversy, 24-year-old Mohammad Elham Yahya Al Maliki, heads a religious forum in East Java’s Kediri regency. In one widely circulated video posted on November 5, he is seen embracing a young girl and sucking in her cheeks. Other clips show him kissing several children on the cheek.
His behaviour drew swift condemnation from rights advocates and religious figures alike. “Elham’s conduct tarnishes the values of da’wah [Islamic preaching] itself, which should provide an example through its attitude and behaviour to the community,” Alissa Wahid, co-chair of the executive board of Nahdlatul Ulama, the country’s largest Muslim organisation, said in a statement on November 12.

Women’s Empowerment and Child Protection Minister Arifah Fauzi warned that such acts could have long-lasting consequences.
“Physical contact without consent, especially when perpetrated by an adult against a child … can have serious psychological impacts on the victim,” she said.
The National Commission on Child Protection is investigating the incident to determine whether it constitutes a criminal offence. The country’s 2022 law on sexual crimes includes non-physical sexual harassment among nine categories of punishable offences.
On November 12, Elham issued an apology for the “furore caused by the viral videos”, insisting the girls “were under the supervision of their parents who attended my regular religious study group”.
Long-standing debate
The push for preacher certification is not new in Indonesia. It first emerged in 2017, when the Ministry of Religious Affairs proposed vetting Friday sermons ahead of the 2019 general elections to curb sectarian rhetoric and avoid inflaming political tensions.
In 2018, the ministry released a list of 200 muballighs or da’i (Muslim preachers) that they saw as “moderate” and steered clear from radical and intolerant sermons.
In 2019, the ministry tapped the Indonesian Council of Ulema (MUI) and Nahdlatul Ulama to launch a voluntary certification programme for da’i, which remains to this day. As of November 2, more than 4,000 preachers have been certified by MUI, according to a statement on its website.
Calls for mandatory certification for preachers resurfaced in December last year after Miftah Maulana, a da’i and then special envoy of President Prabowo Subianto, received flak for publicly undermining a tea seller at an event in Central Java. He later stepped down from his position.

The controversy surrounding Elham has boosted calls for a certification programme for all muballighs, with the hope of increasing the quality of Muslim preachers and preventing future inappropriate conduct.
“This incident demonstrates the urgency to strengthen the certification of preachers without reducing freedom of preaching,” Singgih Januratmoko, a lawmaker with Golkar Party, told news magazine Tempo on November 13.
Social media users also voiced support for the idea.
“Religious leaders or preachers, regardless of their beliefs, must have their qualifications and competencies strictly regulated, equipped with a special independent authority that oversees and has the power to take firm steps to prevent and mitigate things like this,” one user said.
Sisters in Danger, a pop-rock band that promotes women protection, said certification could boost credibility for preachers and provide security for congregants.
“Ethical standards, gender sensitivity understanding, as well as sanction mechanisms, if there are violations, would make worship spaces … remain safe for everyone,” the band said on social media.
The certification could also help preachers retain their livelihood. Muhammad Cholil Nafis, head of the da’wah and community development commission at MUI, said in August that clerics who were “initially banned in Brunei, Singapore, and even Bali” were finally allowed to preach after gaining official recognition through MUI’s certification programme.

However, some Islamic groups might oppose certification due to “lack of clarity” regarding the purpose and standard being used in the programme, said Mohammad Iqbal Ahnaf, a lecturer of philosophy and religious studies at Gadjah Mada University.
“It is somewhat difficult to measure the quality or depth of knowledge of preachers, especially in the Indonesian context. This is different from other Muslim countries, where they have strong control over who can preach,” Mohammad told This Week in Asia.
“In Indonesia, religious organisations are diverse, there is a free market of [Islamic studies]. An ulama is not determined by a single institution. Ulama is a social title, so their existence depends greatly on the extent to which they are accepted by society. So far, I have not seen that the certification programme will have the expected positive impact.”
According to Mohammad, the Indonesian government “does not have the authority” to determine who has the title of ulama.
Syafiq Hasyim, an associate professor at the Indonesian International Islamic University, said some Muslim groups were “worried that the certification is used for other purposes, such as selecting preachers who can become mouthpieces for the state and rulers, or as tools for [spreading] an ideology”.
Should the government go ahead with the certification, Syafiq urged them to be clear about the purpose and maintain a channel to communicate with da’wah associations.
“The certification should also be carried out by an independent institution, selected from various expert groups, including ulama, academics, the professional world, and those seen as enemies of religious figures,” he said.

data.title: Beijing’s rare earth control pause is ‘opportunity’ to mend China-EU ties, envoy says
data.description:
China’s suspension of sweeping rare earth export controls has lent “new stability” to its ties with the European Union, the bloc’s ambassador to China has said, suggesting that the two sides now have a window to improve frosty relations.
Speaking at a forum in Beijing on Thursday, Jorge Toledo initially offered a bleak assessment of the EU’s “difficult” relationship with China even after the high-profile summit in July between European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and Chinese President Xi Jinping.
The two sides had agreed on a new mechanism to help smooth the export of rare earth elements and magnets during the meeting, with von der Leyen describing trade ties as having “reached an inflection point”.
The EU and China still “remain at an inflection point”, Toledo told the forum. “Since the summit, things have not improved. Things have been difficult because of many factors but especially when it comes to supply chains [and] export controls,” he said.


But in a sign of optimism, Toledo added that both sides had an “opportunity” to repair ties after Beijing agreed to pause the expansion of its export controls over rare earths for one year.
China in October announced an expansion of its rare earth restrictions but then suspended them later that month after talks with US President Donald Trump in South Korea.
The EU described the decision as an “appropriate and responsible step in the context of ensuring stable global trade flows in a critically important area”.
“China confirmed that the suspension of the October export controls applies to the EU. Both sides reaffirmed commitments to continue engagement on improving the implementation of export control policies,” EU Trade Commissioner Maros Sefcovic said.
“Now we have an opportunity because of the recent suspension of the October export controls. This is good news,” Toledo said.
“Now things have stabilised. We have one year. Let’s build on that to make progress on EU-China relations, which is so consequential.”
“Let me express my wish … [to] start dialogue again and improve all the issues in which we must make progress,” he added.
Relations between the EU and Beijing have remained tense over several issues, including critical mineral supplies, security concerns, Chinese manufacturing overcapacity and Beijing’s position on the war in Ukraine.
Toledo also dismissed Chinese reports critical of the EU which suggested that the bloc was the “problem” and that China’s ties with individual European countries were more positive. “The EU is nothing different from the union of its member states,” he said.
“If the European Union approves listings or sanctions, it is – and in this case very clearly – with the unanimity of its member states,” Toledo said. “We have, in the European Union, no desire for bad relations with China.”
“We have all desires for partnership, for engagement and for dialogue. But ignoring that there is a political union, that there is a political will and that there is a union behind all we do would be risky and would not be conducive to anything good,” he added.
The European envoy was speaking at a round table on global governance and the United Nations as part of a forum organised by the Centre for China and Globalisation, a non-governmental think tank.
Toledo said the UN was grappling with multiple challenges, the biggest of which was that “one of the five permanent members of the Security Council started an aggression … against a smaller country”, referring to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.
“The solution [to] this conflict, which we hope will come soon, could be the basis of a renewed UN system,” he added.
Other ambassadors attending the round table also urged changes to the UN, amid growing calls globally for reform due to its inability to address ongoing conflicts, while developing economies call for greater representation.
Germany’s ambassador to China, Patricia Flor, said that while the world has benefited massively from the UN over the last 80 years, it was true that “the structures created in 1945 do not reflect the world of today”.
“It’s as simple as that. That means that reform of the different UN institutions is urgently required … The question is how do we preserve the founding principles [of] inclusion [and] consensus-oriented decision making while strengthening regional representation, for instance of Africans,” she said.
Selcuk Unal, Turkey’s ambassador to China, added that reform of the UN Security Council was an “obvious necessity”, and that Turkey supported a broader representation and a rotational system.
“The system is not working, unfortunately,” he said.

data.title: Hong Kong football chief backs under-fire boss Westwood after Singapore defeat, fans’ anger
data.description:
Hong Kong football chief Pui Kwan-kay implied on Thursday that he expects Ashley Westwood to remain in his job as manager of the city’s representative team, despite the continued fallout following the defeat to Singapore.
Westwood has been the target of fierce supporter criticism since Tuesday’s crushing Asian Cup loss.
And in another blow to the national team, in the latest Fifa rankings, issued on Thursday, Hong Kong slipped five places to No 153, while Singapore climbed four positions to No 151.
As hordes of fans called for the Englishman to be axed, Rosanna Law Shuk-pui, the city’s sports minister, weighed in, too, writing on social media on Wednesday that “many fans, including myself, have opinions that differ significantly from coach Westwood’s plans!”.
Pui, who is midway through a four-year term, refused to respond to Law’s remarks but said he would not bow to pressure applied by officials’ comments.

Westwood has twice survived periods of supporter unrest but this week’s messy defeat at Kai Tak Stadium, which sent Singapore to the 2027 Asian Cup at the expense of Hong Kong, has turned up the heat like never before.
The 49-year-old selected the erratic Wang Zhenpeng in goal, a bewildering decision that backfired when the 41-year-old hung back on his line to allow Shawal Anuar the freedom of Kai Tak to score Singapore’s second-half equaliser.
Westwood was also criticised for delaying his first substitutions until the 71st minute, by which time Singapore substitute Ilhan Fandi had fired his side into the lead.
The two players taken off were Fernando and Stefan Pereira, aged 39 and 37, respectively, and Westwood acknowledged he had started the pair knowing they would not complete 90 minutes.
Pui said he had noted some players were tiring after half-time and that many supporters thought the changes should have come earlier.
Westwood sent on striker Michael Udebuluzor and defender Dudu for seven added minutes, but left a raft of creative talent twiddling its thumbs on the bench.
Regardless, Pui said Westwood would not be judged on one match, and that his performance would be monitored over time.
Hong Kong are not scheduled to play again until they meet India in what is a now dead-rubber Asian qualifier on March 31 next year. Westwood said that match remained vital, because the city’s team’s global ranking would determine the strength of their qualifying group for the 2030 World Cup.
Law said that although the game was “only for formality” she believed the Hong Kong team would “go all out in the spirit of sportsmanship and to thank the fans for their loyal support”.
Following his appointment in August last year, Westwood has won 10 and drawn six of his 20 matches.
A rocky start in charge, when he railed against fans’ disapproval after a defeat in Liechtenstein, gave way to a 10-match unbeaten run and an accompanying soaring popularity rating for the boss.
However, a run of only two wins in nine games, culminating with the disastrous Singapore loss, has consigned those happier times to history.
In June, Westwood accused Hong Kong fans who opposed his decision to exclude striker Lau Chi-lok from his squad of “knowing nothing about football”.
Hostilities were halted after a home qualifying win over India, but the gloves are back off after this week’s debacle.

data.title: China woman makes dresses from peanut shells, corn kernels, turning farmland into catwalk
data.description:
A rural Chinese woman has gone viral online for her creativity, transforming farmland into a dazzling catwalk by designing dresses from natural materials such as peanut shells, corn leaves, red beans, sweet potatoes, and corn kernels.
The blogger, known online as “Village Magical Treasure,” boasts over 152,000 followers on Douyin and hails from Hengzhou in Guangxi province, southern China.
In her videos, she artfully uses materials like peanut shells, dried grass, corn kernels, and corn husks to craft unique, rural-inspired clothing.
She models her creations confidently, strutting through fields and showcasing what she proudly dubs “countryside fashion.”
So far, she has posted 178 original videos.

In one viral video from August, she crafted a dress entirely out of peanut shells, paired it with a red bean “necktie,” and walked through the fields. The clip garnered over 180,000 likes.
She has also launched several vegetable-themed fashion shows, featuring outfits such as the “sweet potato dress” and the “long bean outfit.”
In a more recent video, she created an elaborate dragon robe from corn leaves, complete with a crown made of corn kernels, while reciting lines from the popular 2015 Chinese TV drama The Legend of Mi Yue.

“Every piece is crafted by me. The patterns and styles reflect my hobbies and preferences. All the fruits and vegetables used were grown by the elderly members of my family. I hope that, with this attention, I can help promote local agricultural products from my hometown,” she shared.
In an interview with Chongqing Broadcasting Group, the blogger revealed that she has never received any professional training in art or design.
“This is simply a personal hobby. I’m not a professional designer and have never undergone formal training. All the materials come from what we grow in the countryside,” she explained.

“I’ve loved making clothes from different materials since childhood, always using eco-friendly items like vegetables or recyclable plastic bags. Whenever I see materials in abundance, I feel inspired to create outfits. Some are suitable for sewing, others for gluing, and I use a variety of tools depending on the material,” she added.
Her creativity has sparked lively discussions across social media, with many online viewers in China marvelling at her talent.
One commenter joked: “I crown you the Corn Empress. You are the ruler of all corn.”
Another noted: “Each piece is a one-of-a-kind masterpiece. This isn’t farming; it’s pure art.”
A third remarked: “She is so creative. This deserves a Guinness World Record!”

data.title: 7 most creative new jewellery drops, from Stray Kids’ Bang Chan in Fendi to Chanel and Prada
data.description:
This season, leading jewellers are reimagining modern adornment. Creativity takes centre stage with fresh designs from brands like Boucheron and Van Cleef & Arpels. Meanwhile, Collina Strada collaborates with Awe Inspired as Prada releases colourful gems, and Chanel’s classic couture braid is reinvented as jewellery.
Collina Strada

New York-based fashion label Collina Strada, helmed by designer Hillary Taymour, and jewellery label Awe Inspired have joined forces for a series of striking pieces that debuted at the brand’s spring/summer 2026 show during New York Fashion Week. Highlights include the Twisted Thorn earring, Hedgehog pendant and ring, and Gua Sha pendant.
Prada

Fans of coloured gemstones should check out Prada’s Couleur Vivante fine jewellery collection. The range stands out for its vivid hues and unexpected colour combinations, achieved through the pairing of stones ranging from amethyst to aquamarine, citrine, morganite and peridot.
Chanel

Chanel’s couture braid, used as trim on its iconic jackets, becomes a piece of jewellery in the Première Galon, a new version of the Première, the house’s first watch for women, introduced back in 1987. The watch’s bracelet is braided out of yellow gold, and is also available in a version embellished with diamonds.
Fendi

Made entirely of sterling silver, Fendi 925 is a new men’s jewellery line from the Roman house. Part of the brand’s centenary collection this year – hence the name – the range, which has already been worn by K-pop sensation Bang Chan, features designs such as chain necklaces, signet rings and a mono earring adorned with the brand’s FF logo.
Homer

Homer, American singer Frank Ocean’s luxury line, is in expansion mode with store openings in London and Los Angeles. The brand’s colourful and cool creations include Bullet Hole Heart pendants – which come in vivid colours – and the chain-link Sodium collection.
Boucheron

Boucheron’s Quatre needs no introduction. Now, building on that signature range, the brand has added the Quatre Sand collection. Combining invention and craftsmanship, the bracelets and bangles in this collection offer a striking contrast with their mix of the usual yellow gold with 3D-printed black sand.
Van Cleef & Arpels

First created in 1968, Van Cleef & Arpels’ Alhambra has become one of the jewellery world’s most iconic – and most imitated – motifs. Now, for the first time, the maison has introduced transformable creations. This necklace, for instance, can be worn long, short and even as a bracelet thanks to a detachable element. Made of rose gold, grey mother-of-pearl and white mother-of-pearl, this piece also stands out for its asymmetrical arrangement of the Alhambra motif, which appears in different sizes and materials, including guilloché gold.
Additional contributions by Sumnima Kandangwa

data.title: How does a guest shift really work? Hong Kong bartenders spill the tea
data.description:
Hong Kong enjoys a rarefied status as one of the most drinks-literate cities in the world, being home to Bar Leone (which topped the World’s 50 Best Bars list this year) as well as numerous other internationally acclaimed establishments. When the city hosted the World’s 50 Best Bars ceremony in October, the weeks either side of the ceremony saw almost every major bar in Hong Kong host a guest mixologist or a team from cities as nearby as Singapore and Seoul and from as far away as Buenos Aires, Athens and Albuquerque. For guests, these are opportunities to sample a new concept, an approach to cocktails or unique styles of service.
The high skill level of the city’s bar professionals allows for such a high frequency of guest shifts, but this may be a blessing and a curse. Hong Kong was virtually drowning in guest shifts during the week of World’s 50 Best Bars, multiplying the logistics of any single guest shift over consecutive nights in a citywide juggling act leading up to announcement day on October 8. The Savory Project hosted Sip & Guzzle from New York, then a 50 Best Bars Session titled “Off Menu”, before hosting now-World’s 2nd Best Bar Handshake Speakeasy before the big day. Courtroom hosted Barro Negro Athens and the World of Mixologists collective on October 6, before Shinji’s NYC took over early evening on October 7.

“Hongkongers might take for granted just how vibrant and internationally connected the city’s bar scene is,” says Gennaro Pucci, bar manager of Qura Bar at the Regent Hong Kong. “Guest shifts are a regular occurrence here – sometimes happening weekly at popular venues.”
But this bustling calendar of events masks a hidden reality: guest shifts are a logistical marathon, which, despite their frequency, require immense preparation every time.
Pucci explains that it all starts with agreeing on a date, the style of the event, which sponsor brand they can bring on board, ingredient logistics and fees. In recent months, Qura Bar has hosted a wide array of concepts, ranging from Bacchanalia London on October 26, which focused on art-inspired cocktails, to Noble Roots Bar from Serbia, which focused on farm-to-table-style cocktails.

At Courtroom, co-founder Saan Dhakal says he and his team lock in their guest shifts abroad at least three months in advance. Such a long lead time is necessary for them to prepare their fermented ingredients that require a large variety of spices and herbs and the use of state-of-the-art equipment. Dhakal carries all essential niche ingredients and tools with him personally to ensure consistency, and always briefs the team on the ingredients and techniques before starting the shift.
Such meticulousness is not uncommon in today’s high-end bar industry, which, as Rolf Flaminiano of Avoca points out, is about much more than slinging three or four off-the-shelf ingredients together in a shaker. “Cocktails today are much more than mixing a few spirits and ingredients,” he explains. “Good drinks nowadays are the result of extensive research and a multitude of processes, including blending, fat-washing, clarifying, infusing, smoking, ageing, distilling and many more techniques. This requires extensive preparation and careful planning when it comes to guest shifts.”

This level of prep is nothing unfamiliar to Amir Javaid, co-founder of SoHo neighbourhood bar Socio. Javaid’s establishment focuses on extracting flavours and building unique concoctions from upcycled ingredients sourced from local venues – such as oyster shells from Caine Road seafood vendor Hooked, or milk from Nordic cafe Venner, which is used to make home-made yogurt – ingredients that must be processed and finalised in Hong Kong and transported to overseas guest shifts. Managing all of this means planning for a guest shift (and a masterclass, if they can arrange it) often requires at least a month for Javaid and team. All of this entails high costs, so it’s no surprise. Javaid says he “usually [brings their] bestsellers” to guest shifts abroad to maximise the return.
“Economically, we often involve brand partners,” adds Dhakal. “Their support is crucial for managing the logistics and costs of bringing in a bartender.”

It’s not just about costs, though. A clear plan is crucial when dealing with the array of challenges posed by any guest shift taking place at scale. “Some drinks may work best in their original setting where mood, environment and even weather play a role,” Flaminiano points out. “While the recipe remains the same, the experience can feel different. We would encourage guests to talk to the bartender to learn the story behind the drink – it adds so much to the experience.”
During the hectic guest shifts Hong Kong witnessed during this year’s World’s 50 Best Bars sessions, that kind of chit chat is easier said than done. Dhakal admits that during an extremely busy shift, it’s difficult for bartenders to have the kinds of long conversations with customers they typically enjoy. “However, if someone mentions they’d like to say hello, we always try to make it happen,” he says, adding that guests shouldn’t worry about questioning bartenders about what they’re making since “they love to share stories” about their creations.

All of the above makes it seem like a guest shift involves a lot of work for the visitors, a situation that only benefits the host venue. But Dhakal is adamant that the value of a guest shift goes both ways. “We learn new techniques, different service styles and new approaches to hospitality. I’ve personally learned so much from guest shifts about different work cultures and how to create a ‘family’ atmosphere. My team is always learning from inbound shifts, which is why we love hosting our friends from different regions,” he says.
Javaid agrees that the hard work is worth it and that there are benefits beyond just the bottom line. “Sometimes the bar team can get a little complacent and bored,” he jokes. “If you work the same bar job five days a week, it gets a little tiresome. But when you do shifts overseas, see the hospitality and how much work people put into an overall guest experience, it makes them realise they still have a long way to go.”
Ultimately, guest shifts are all about “community”, according to Dhakal. “It’s both a learning and networking opportunity, but the community aspect is most important. When a bar from Europe visits Hong Kong, our local guests can experience their cocktails without needing to travel. That’s the beauty of it, and the same is true when our team travels abroad.”

data.title: Tesla rival Xiaomi reaffirms safety focus after raising 2025 sales target to 400,000 units
data.description:
Xiaomi, one of Tesla’s biggest challengers in mainland China over the past year, pledged to focus on the safety and reliability of its cars after its factory reached another milestone.
Founder and CEO Lei Jun said on Thursday that Xiaomi had raised its full-year delivery target from 350,000 vehicles to 400,000 after ramping up production, but stressed that the quality of the SU7 sedan and YU7 SUVs would not be compromised.
“We firmly believe that safety is a foundation and a priority,” he said in a WeChat post. “Based on safety and quality, we are making an all-out effort to accelerate production.”
The remarks came after Xiaomi’s 500,000th EV rolled off its Beijing-based production line on Thursday.

A recent entrant to the country’s automotive industry, Xiaomi delivered its first EV to mainland customers only 19 months ago. However, it has been increasingly viewed as a strong rival to Tesla in the premium EV segment.
The SU7 and YU7 compete in the same segments as Tesla’s Shanghai-made Model 3 and Model Y, causing Elon Musk’s company to lose significant market share in the world’s largest EV market since 2024.
Xiaomi sold 108,796 cars in the third quarter, a jump of 173 per cent from a year earlier and a 33 per cent increase from the preceding three months.
Between January and October, Xiaomi delivered 315,376 cars to Chinese customers, edging closer to meeting its previous full-year target of 350,000.
Tesla, which once accounted for a 16 per cent share of China’s EV market when it started producing locally in 2020, saw its market share slide below 7 per cent last year amid an escalating price war and the rise of domestic rivals.
Its share has fallen to less than 5 per cent this year as Chinese brands from Xiaomi to Leapmotor lured more customers away from the Model 3 and Model Y with their intelligent models featuring advanced driver assistance systems and high-performance batteries.
In June, Xiaomi said it had received 200,000 pre-orders online for its second model, the YU7, within three minutes of launch. That number rose to 240,000 within the first 18 hours. Such enthusiasm for a new EV is rare in China, where monthly sales of 10,000 units for a single model is typically seen as a success.
“Lei Jun’s promise to ensure safety and reliability reflects his concerns after two fatal accidents raised doubts from the public about Xiaomi cars’ quality,” said Chen Jinzhu, CEO of consultancy Shanghai Mingliang Auto Service. “A carmaker cannot just rely on stylish design and hi-tech to promote sales. It has to also convince consumers about the cars’ safety.”
In March, three people died in an accident involving an SU7 in Tongling, central Anhui province, with the vehicle’s driver assistance system turned on. The preliminary self-driving system alerted the driver to take control just two seconds before the EV crashed into a barrier at high speed.
The crash prompted Chinese authorities to tighten their oversight of early-stage autonomous driving technologies.
Last month, another accident involving an SU7 Ultra model killed a 31-year-old man who was suspected of drink-driving in Chengdu, southwestern Sichuan province. According to local media reports, passers-by tried to pull him out of the burning car, but were unable to open the car doors.

data.title: China’s top science academy chief to promote plenum’s spirit at Hong Kong seminar
data.description:
The president of mainland China’s premier science institution will host a seminar in Hong Kong next week to “study and implement” the spirit of the fourth plenum, which sets the tone for the ruling Communist Party’s Central Committee’s policymaking over the next five years.
A Hong Kong government spokesman on Thursday said the seminar, to be held next Tuesday would promote the spirit of the fourth plenary session of the party’s 20th Central Committee among various sectors of the community.
Speakers will include Hou Jianguo, president of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, and Zhu Weidong, deputy director of the Office of the Central Commission for Financial and Economic Affairs.
“[The recommendations for the 15th five-year plan] adopted at the fourth plenary session of the 20th CPC Central Committee provide a top-level design and strategic blueprint for the country’s development over the next five years and are of great significance and impact to Hong Kong,” the spokesman said.
“All citizens of Hong Kong should earnestly study and implement the spirit of the fourth plenary session.”

The Communist Party has set a number of medium- and long-term goals as benchmarks for the country’s economic and social progress.
The fourth plenum proposed the core features of the coming 15th five-year plan, which covers the years from 2026 to 2030.
Beijing has vowed to make “notable progress” in the pursuit of development and to maintain its status as a manufacturing powerhouse while warnings of “raging storms” and growing uncertainties alongside declarations of confidence in the nation’s economic resilience.
A 5,000-word communique issued after the plenum in October called for “further deepening reforms across the board” and continued efforts to improve people’s living standards.
Beijing’s liaison office in Hong Kong has pledged to support the city’s integration into national development plans.
The head of the central government’s liaison office, Zhou Ji, met heads of various departments of the office and representatives of other Beijing arms in Hong Kong last month to lay out the work requirements for the city.
The office said the next five years would be a crucial period for promoting high-quality economic and social development in Hong Kong and playing a better role in building a strong country and national rejuvenation.

data.title: Year of the Snake 10th month predictions for all 12 Chinese zodiac signs. Is your luck in?
data.description:
As the weather cools down in the 10th lunar month, everyone should pay attention to their health, regardless of which of the 12 Chinese zodiac signs they were born under.
Read on to see what feng shui master Andrew Kwan says about your birth year.
Rat (1936, 1948, 1960, 1972, 1984, 1996, 2008, 2020)

Rats will have a volatile month at work. On one hand, teamwork will alleviate work pressure, but on the other, rumours and gossip may surface within the team, so it is advisable to carefully consolidate the existing team and resolve internal conflicts.
In terms of relationships, single Rats will have more social engagements this month. It is best to keep an open mind and engage with different kinds of people. Those in relationships need to see things from their partner’s point of view, as sensitive topics could cause conflict.
Health-wise, there is a risk of lower back and kidney problems this month; take precautions to protect your back and avoid injury.
Ox (1937, 1949, 1961, 1973, 1985, 1997, 2009, 2021)

This month, those born in the Year of the Ox will have good career prospects, with the help of male benefactors. Those working in fields involving new development, problem-solving or creative work will have excellent opportunities to shine.
Single Oxen will find themselves lucky in love, so it is a good time to be bold and make the first move. Those in relationships should avoid neglecting their partners due to work commitments.
Regarding health, those with bone or joint problems may experience a recurrence; stretching and health-promoting exercises are recommended.
Tiger (1938, 1950, 1962, 1974, 1986, 1998, 2010, 2022)

This month, Tigers will have good career prospects, especially those engaged in artistic or creative work. A surge of inspiration and creative bursts will allow projects to progress smoothly.
Singletons may have opportunities to develop romantic feelings for someone through work, and those in relationships are advised to establish shared interests to strengthen their long-term bond.
Regarding health, pay attention to gastrointestinal health, avoid alcohol and maintain a light diet.
Rabbit (1939, 1951, 1963, 1975, 1987, 1999, 2011, 2023)

Challenges lie ahead in the workplace for Rabbits this month. While there will be help from benefactors, be wary of potential problems arising from contract terms, as the devil is in the details.
Single Rabbits may meet someone they are attracted to at work, while those in relationships should show more care and attention to their partners to strengthen their bond.
Rabbits are likely to experience eye problems this month; maintaining a regular sleep schedule is recommended to protect your vision.
Dragon (1940, 1952, 1964, 1976, 1988, 2000, 2012, 2024)

This month, Dragons will enjoy favourable career prospects, with opportunities to be given real authority and make significant progress in their work. However, work pressure will also be considerable, so careful task allocation is crucial to avoid being overwhelmed.
Single Dragons can attract potential partners by exploring their areas of interest, while those in relationships need to communicate more to avoid neglecting their partners amid busy work schedules.
Dragons should pay attention to stress management and liver and gallbladder issues, as problems caused by overwork can be avoided.
Snake (1929, 1941, 1953, 1965, 1977, 1989, 2001, 2013)

Snakes will experience a significant rise in their career prospects in the 10th lunar month, but there will also be numerous challenges at work, some of which could potentially even require crisis management. With crisis comes opportunity, but still, it is a time to tread carefully.
Single Snakes may meet someone while engaging in outdoor activities or extreme sports. Those in relationships may find themselves to be moody, but it is crucial to listen to your partner’s feelings to avoid misunderstandings.
This is a good month for Snakes to get checked for cardiovascular issues and take extra care on the road.
Horse (1930, 1942, 1954, 1966, 1978, 1990, 2002, 2014)

Horses will receive help from a female benefactor at work. It is a good time to focus on existing projects and improve efficiency. However, avoid blindly expanding into high-risk investments to prevent a reversal of fortune.
Single Horses should attend more social events, while those in relationships should be mindful of fulfilling promises they have made to their partners.
This is the month for Horses to take their vitamins, pay attention to weather changes and wear appropriate clothing to avoid frequent minor illnesses.
Sheep (1931, 1943, 1955, 1967, 1979, 1991, 2003, 2015)

Those born in the Year of the Sheep are in for a challenging month. Although external help may be found, overcoming these obstacles will be difficult and will require patience, perseverance and diligence.
This is the month for singles to take a break from dating and concentrate on themselves, while those in relationships need to communicate more to avoid frequent misunderstandings.
Exercise is recommended for Sheep, as is maintaining a healthy diet to prevent gastrointestinal problems.
Monkey (1932, 1944, 1956, 1968, 1980, 1992, 2004, 2016)

Monkeys will face a month of change. Challenges lie ahead at work, and only by working with your team can you turn adversity into an opportunity.
Those involved in international trade should consider expanding their business overseas.
Monkeys looking for relationships will have better luck abroad, too. Those in relationships should manage their emotions to avoid unnecessary conflict with their partners.
Attention should be paid to respiratory issues as the weather cools down in the Northern Hemisphere.
Rooster (1933, 1945, 1957, 1969, 1981, 1993, 2005, 2017)

Those born in the Year of the Rooster will experience mixed fortunes in their careers this month. They will perform well and expand their network, but there is also a risk of being targeted by malicious people and causing trouble. It is advisable to take a breath before dealing with matters to minimise friction.
Relationships are a different matter this month, as single Roosters will have the potential to go on many dates, but should make wise choices if they are seeking a long-term partner.
There is an increased risk for Roosters to experience problems with their voice and vocal cords this month, so limit your intake of spicy and stimulating foods.
Dog (1934, 1946, 1958, 1970, 1982, 1994, 2006, 2018)

Dogs have stable career prospects in the 10th lunar month. Long-standing issues will be resolved, but be cautious about the details of all agreements.
In relationships, singles may need to patiently observe whether a new partner is suitable. Those in relationships can establish shared habits, which will greatly help build a solid foundation in the relationship.
Sleep may be a key focus for Dogs this month. It is recommended to get plenty of rest. Soaking your feet at night may be helpful.
Pig (1935, 1947, 1959, 1971, 1983, 1995, 2007, 2019)

This month is a good time for Pigs to embrace new beginnings and take on new tasks and projects. However, you may suddenly discover accumulated debts that need to be paid, so you need to control your spending.
In terms of relationships, singles may encounter a charming person but find they are commitment-shy. Those in relationships should be aware that old problems may resurface and require patient resolution.
The liver and kidneys may be under some strain for Pigs; it is recommended to maintain a light diet and drink plenty of water to stay healthy.
